label: Guatemala City — 1954 Coup
sub: CIA Operation PBSUCCESS overthrew elected President Jacobo Árbenz on June 27, 1954. The coup ended Guatemala's democratic decade and ushered in 40+ years of military rule and civil war.

label: Havana — US Occupations & Embargo
sub: Cuba was occupied by US forces 1898–1902 and 1906–1909 under the Platt Amendment. The US trade embargo, in place since 1960, is the world's longest-standing trade embargo.

label: Playa Girón — Bay of Pigs 1961
sub: CIA-trained Brigade 2506 (1,400 Cuban exiles) landed here on April 17, 1961. Within 72 hours the invasion was crushed: 114 killed, 1,189 captured. A defining failure for the CIA and Kennedy administration.

label: Santiago — Pinochet Coup Sep 11, 1973
sub: Chilean military forces bombed La Moneda Palace and overthrew Salvador Allende on September 11, 1973. CIA covert operations (Tracks I & II) had worked for three years to destabilize Allende's government.

label: La Moneda Palace — Allende's Last Stand
sub: Chilean Air Force jets bombed La Moneda presidential palace during the September 11, 1973 coup. President Allende died inside during the assault. The palace became a symbol of US-backed authoritarianism in Latin America.

label: Managua — Sandinistas vs. Contras
sub: Capital of Nicaragua during the Contra war (1981–1990). The CIA-backed Contra rebels launched attacks from Honduras and Costa Rica. The Sandinista government survived but suffered 30,000+ deaths in the conflict.

label: Panama City — Operation Just Cause 1989
sub: 27,000 US troops invaded Panama on December 20, 1989 to capture General Noriega. The El Chorrillo neighborhood was largely destroyed by fire during the invasion. Civilian death toll disputed: 202–1,000+.

label: El Chorrillo — Neighborhood Destroyed (1989)
sub: Working-class Panama City neighborhood near the Defense Forces headquarters. Largely destroyed by fire during Operation Just Cause. 15,000+ residents made homeless. Some estimates attribute the fire to US operations.

label: Port-au-Prince — US Occupations 1915–1934, 1994, 2004
sub: Haiti was occupied by US Marines 1915–1934, restoring democracy in 1994 (Operation Uphold Democracy), and again saw US-facilitated regime change in 2004. The CIA also backed the FRAPH paramilitary during 1991–1994 junta rule.

label: Buenos Aires — Dirty War & Operation Condor
sub: Center of Argentina's military dictatorship (1976–1983). 30,000 people disappeared. The ESMA (Navy Petty Officers' School) served as a major torture and detention center; 5,000+ detained there, many thrown into the Río de la Plata.

label: ESMA — Argentina's Most Notorious Torture Center
sub: The Navy Petty-Officers School of Mechanics (ESMA) held an estimated 5,000+ detainees during Argentina's Dirty War. Prisoners were tortured and many were killed in 'death flights'—thrown into the Río de la Plata from aircraft. Now a Museum of Memory.

label: Bogotá — Plan Colombia Operations
sub: Colombia received over $10 billion in US security aid since 2000 under Plan Colombia. The program combined counternarcotics operations with counterinsurgency against FARC. Aerial fumigation affected hundreds of thousands of hectares of farmland.

label: Tegucigalpa — Honduras as US Military Platform
sub: Honduras was converted into the primary US military platform for Contra operations. Battalion 3-16, armed with US support, disappeared 184 people. US military presence grew from 25 to 1,200 soldiers; Palmerola Air Base became a key logistics hub.

label: San Salvador — US Aid & Death Squads
sub: The US provided $6 billion in military and economic aid to El Salvador during the 1979–1992 civil war. US-trained Atlacatl Battalion committed the El Mozote massacre (1981). Archbishop Romero was assassinated in March 1980.

label: El Mozote — Massacre Site 1981
sub: The US-trained Atlacatl Battalion massacred approximately 1,000 civilians at El Mozote, El Salvador, in December 1981. Over 553 victims were children. The Reagan administration initially denied the massacre; the UN Truth Commission confirmed it in 1993.

label: Santo Domingo — US Intervention 1965
sub: 22,000 US troops (Operation Power Pack) invaded the Dominican Republic on April 28, 1965 to prevent a constitutional government from taking power. The intervention kept US-backed Balaguer in power for decades.

label: Caracas — US-Backed Coup Attempt 2002
sub: Hugo Chávez was briefly overthrown in a coup on April 11, 2002; the Bush administration recognized the new government and praised the 'democratic change.' Chávez was restored within 47 hours after mass popular protests. US funding of opposition groups was confirmed through NED and USAID documents.

label: Guantánamo Bay — US Naval Base Since 1903
sub: The US obtained Guantánamo Bay under the 1903 Cuban-American Treaty and retained it after abrogating the Platt Amendment in 1934. Cuba has demanded its return since 1959. After 9/11 it became notorious as a detention and interrogation facility for terrorism suspects.

label: Fort Gulick — School of the Americas (1946–1984)
sub: The US Army's School of the Americas operated at Fort Gulick, Panama Canal Zone, from 1946 to 1984, training Latin American military officers in counterinsurgency. Many graduates became notorious human rights violators. The school moved to Fort Benning, Georgia in 1984.

label: Retalhuleu — CIA Training Base for Bay of Pigs
sub: The CIA established a secret training facility (code-named JMTrax) at Retalhuleu airport in Guatemala to train Cuban exiles for the Bay of Pigs invasion. The base was built with Guatemalan President Ydigoras Fuentes's cooperation, trained approximately 2,000 men.

label: Estadio Nacional — Mass Detention Center 1973
sub: Santiago's National Stadium was used as a mass detention and torture center following Pinochet's 1973 coup. An estimated 40,000 people passed through Chilean detention centers; folk singer Víctor Jara was tortured and killed here.

label: Villa Grimaldi — DINA Torture Center, Santiago
sub: Villa Grimaldi was the main detention and torture center of DINA (Chilean secret police) during Pinochet's dictatorship. Approximately 4,500 people were held there; around 240 were killed. Now a Peace Park and museum.

label: Montevideo — Uruguay Military Dictatorship
sub: Uruguay's military dictatorship (1973–1985) used CIA-trained techniques to systematically torture political prisoners. Uruguay had the world's highest per-capita rate of political prisoners in the late 1970s. The US provided Dan Mitrione, who taught torture methods to Uruguayan police.

label: Asunción — Paraguay: Condor Archive Found
sub: Paraguay's secret police archives—discovered in 1992 by human rights lawyer Martín Almada—provided documentary proof of Operation Condor's coordination among military dictatorships. The 'Terror Archive' documents are now a UNESCO Memory of the World heritage site.

label: La Higuera, Bolivia — Che Guevara Executed 1967
sub: CIA officer Félix Rodríguez and US-trained Bolivian Special Forces captured Ernesto 'Che' Guevara at La Higuera on October 8, 1967. Guevara was executed the next day per orders of the Bolivian government. Rodríguez later confirmed his CIA role in the operation.

label: La Paz — Bolivia CIA-Backed Coups
sub: Bolivia experienced multiple US-linked coups. In 1971, General Hugo Banzer overthrew socialist President Juan José Torres with reported CIA support. In 2019, President Evo Morales resigned after the military withdrew support following an OAS election report now disputed by independent analysts.

label: Brasília — Brazil Military Coup 1964
sub: The US supported the military coup that overthrew President João Goulart on April 1, 1964. Ambassador Lincoln Gordon recognized the military government immediately. Operation Brother Sam positioned US naval forces offshore. The Brazilian military dictatorship lasted 21 years.

label: Rio de Janeiro — Brazil Coup Command Center 1964
sub: Brazilian military commanders coordinated the 1964 coup against President Goulart from Rio de Janeiro. US naval forces (Operation Brother Sam) were positioned 400 miles offshore to provide support if needed. The coup installed a military government that lasted until 1985.

label: Santiago de Cuba — Spanish-American War 1898
sub: US forces defeated the Spanish fleet at the Battle of Santiago Bay (July 3, 1898) and took the city, effectively ending the Spanish-American War. Theodore Roosevelt's Rough Riders charged San Juan Hill nearby. Cuba came under US military occupation.

label: San Juan, Puerto Rico — Annexed 1898
sub: US forces occupied Puerto Rico during the Spanish-American War. Puerto Rico became an unincorporated US territory by the Treaty of Paris (December 1898). Puerto Ricans were not granted US citizenship until 1917 and still cannot vote in presidential elections.

label: Veracruz, Mexico — US Occupation 1914
sub: US forces occupied Veracruz, Mexico from April to November 1914 following the 'Tampico Affair.' 19 Americans and 126–172 Mexicans died. The occupation was widely condemned in Latin America as a violation of sovereignty and deepened regional anti-US sentiment.

label: Cap-Haïtien — Caco Resistance vs. US Marines
sub: Northern Haiti was the center of the Caco resistance movement against US occupation (1915–1934). Charlemagne Péralte led the 1918–1920 uprising from here. US Marines killed Péralte in 1919 and photographed his body displayed on a cross—an image that became iconic in Haitian nationalism.

label: Managua — Somoza Dynasty (1936–1979)
sub: The Somoza family ruled Nicaragua for 43 years with consistent US backing. Anastasio Somoza García (assassinated 1956), Luis Somoza, and Anastasio Somoza Debayle were all maintained in power through US military and economic support. FDR reportedly said: 'He may be an S.O.B., but he's our S.O.B.'

label: Tegucigalpa — Zelaya Coup 2009
sub: Honduran President Manuel Zelaya was seized by the military at gunpoint on June 28, 2009 and flown to Costa Rica. The Obama administration initially called it a coup but Secretary Clinton worked to facilitate elections legitimizing the new government rather than restoring Zelaya.

label: Palmerola (Soto Cano) Air Base — Honduras
sub: Palmerola Air Base became a key US military logistics hub in the 1980s for Contra supply operations. US forces used it for joint exercises and as a staging point for operations in El Salvador and Nicaragua. It remains an active US military facility.

label: St. George's, Grenada — Operation Urgent Fury 1983
sub: US forces invaded Grenada on October 25, 1983, with approximately 7,000 troops. Reagan cited danger to American medical students as justification. The UN General Assembly condemned the invasion 108–9 as a 'flagrant violation of international law.'

label: Northern Costa Rica — Contra Southern Front
sub: The CIA established a second Contra front in Costa Rica operating from the northern border region. Costa Rican territory was used for Contra logistics despite the country's neutrality. This secondary front was run by CIA officer John Hull through a network of farms.

label: Las Vegas (Honduras border) — CIA Contra Base
sub: The CIA constructed a secret training facility near Las Vegas, Honduras, close to the Nicaraguan border. Contras were trained, armed, and supplied from bases throughout Honduras with US military and CIA coordination.

label: Lima — US Counternarcotics Operations Peru
sub: The US engaged in extensive counternarcotics and counterinsurgency cooperation with Peru, including during the Shining Path (Sendero Luminoso) period. US-backed fumigation and eradication programs displaced Andean farming communities. DEA operations expanded through the 1990s.

label: Quito — Ecuador: Manta Air Base (1999–2009)
sub: The US operated a Forward Operating Location (FOL) at Manta Air Base in Ecuador from 1999 to 2009 for counternarcotics surveillance missions. President Rafael Correa refused to renew the base agreement in 2009, stating: 'We'll renew if the US lets Ecuador put a base in Miami.'

label: Asunción — Stroessner Dictatorship 1954–1989
sub: General Alfredo Stroessner ruled Paraguay for 35 years with US support. Paraguay was a founding member of Operation Condor and harbored fugitive Nazis including Josef Mengele. The Terror Archives found in 1992 document Condor coordination throughout South America.

label: Buenos Aires — Condor Phase 3 Operations
sub: Buenos Aires served as a key operational hub for Operation Condor's transnational assassination campaign. Uruguayan, Chilean, and Bolivian exiles were among those disappeared here by Argentine security forces acting on behalf of other Condor member states.

label: Vallegrande — Che Guevara's Body Displayed 1967
sub: After Che Guevara's execution in La Higuera, his body was flown to Vallegrande and displayed at the laundry room of the local hospital for international press. CIA officer Félix Rodríguez photographed the body. Guevara's hands were amputated for fingerprint identification before burial.

label: Washington DC — Letelier Assassination 1976
sub: DINA car bomb killed Chilean exile Orlando Letelier and US citizen Ronni Moffitt on Sheridan Circle, Washington DC, on September 21, 1976. The attack was the worst act of foreign-sponsored terrorism on US soil prior to 9/11. CIA's Michael Townley later confessed to building the bomb.

label: Miraflores Palace — Venezuela Coup Attempts 2002–2019
sub: Miraflores Palace was briefly vacated during the 2002 coup before Chávez's return. In 2019, the US recognized Guaidó as 'interim president' while Maduro retained control of the palace and military. US sanctions targeted the Venezuelan economy to pressure regime change.

label: San Cristóbal — Soviet Missile Sites 1962
sub: Soviet medium-range ballistic missile sites were discovered near San Cristóbal, Cuba by US U-2 reconnaissance on October 14, 1962. The discovery triggered the 13-day Cuban Missile Crisis—the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict. The missiles were removed after US-Soviet negotiations.

label: Caracas — US Sanctions Campaign 2015–Present
sub: The US has imposed escalating economic sanctions on Venezuela since 2015, including an executive order declaring Venezuela a 'national security threat.' A 2019 executive order blocked virtually all economic transactions. The UN Special Rapporteur concluded sanctions violated international law and human rights.

label: Cartagena — Colombia Drug War Operations
sub: Colombia's Caribbean coast was a major node in the drug trafficking networks targeted by US-backed operations. Plan Colombia's naval interdiction operations worked from Caribbean ports. The Andean Regional Initiative expanded US military presence throughout the region.

label: Cochabamba — Water War 2000; IMF Privatization
sub: The 2000 Cochabamba Water War erupted after US-backed IMF structural adjustment required Bolivia to privatize its water supply, contracted to Bechtel Corporation. Protests were suppressed violently, killing a 17-year-old. The IMF/World Bank privatization program was a condition of US aid and loans.

label: Corinto Harbor — CIA Mining 1984
sub: The CIA secretly mined Corinto and other Nicaraguan ports in January 1984 without informing Congress. The mines damaged ships from the Netherlands, Japan, and the Soviet Union. The ICJ ruled the US violated international law and owed Nicaragua reparations—which the US refused to pay.

label: Mexico City — Tlatelolco Massacre 1968
sub: CIA liaison to Mexico's DFS security service had foreknowledge of and may have facilitated the October 2, 1968 Tlatelolco massacre of student protesters (estimates range from 30 to 400 killed). CIA officer Philip Agee documented US intelligence cooperation with Mexico's political repression.

label: La Paz — Bolivia 2019 Coup Controversy
sub: Evo Morales resigned as president on November 10, 2019 after the military withdrew support following an OAS report alleging election fraud. CEPR analysis found the OAS used faulty statistical methodology. Bolivia held new elections in 2020 that Morales's MAS party won decisively.

label: Medellín — Colombian Condor Cooperation
sub: Colombia participated in information-sharing aspects of Operation Condor. Colombian security services (DAS) received CIA training and equipment and cooperated with Condor intelligence networks in tracking left-wing exiles and activists.

label: Havana — Bay of Pigs Prisoner Exchange 1962
sub: 1,189 captured Bay of Pigs invaders were held in Cuban prisons. The Kennedy administration negotiated their release in December 1962 in exchange for $53 million in food and medicine. The prisoner exchange concluded one of the most humiliating chapters in CIA history.

label: San José — Costa Rica: Iran-Contra Operations
sub: Costa Rica was used as a logistics base for Contra operations despite its neutrality. Oliver North's 'Enterprise' network operated a secret airstrip at Santa Elena in northern Costa Rica. Costa Rican President Oscar Arias later played a key role in the Esquipulas peace process.

label: Tegucigalpa — Battalion 3-16 Death Squad
sub: Battalion 3-16 was a Honduran military intelligence unit trained and equipped by the CIA that disappeared 184 people in the 1980s. The unit's existence was first exposed by Baltimore Sun journalists Gary Cohn and Ginger Thompson in 1995. CIA contractors participated in prisoner interrogations.

label: Papal Nunciature — Noriega's Surrender 1990
sub: General Noriega took refuge in the Vatican diplomatic mission in Panama City on December 24, 1989. US forces surrounded the building and played rock music at high volume for days. Noriega surrendered on January 3, 1990. He was flown to Miami, tried, and sentenced to 40 years for drug trafficking.

label: PDVSA HQ — Venezuelan Oil Asset Sanctions
sub: The US sanctioned PDVSA (Venezuela's state oil company) in January 2019, blocking US entities from doing business with it and freezing an estimated $7 billion in assets. Venezuela's US-held oil assets were transferred to Guaidó's parallel government. The sanctions contributed to a sharp drop in Venezuelan oil production.

label: Ixil Triangle — Maya Genocide (1982–1983)
sub: The Ixil region of Guatemala's Quiché department was the center of the Maya genocide carried out by Ríos Montt's military (1982–1983), which Reagan backed with military aid and called 'a man of great personal integrity.' An estimated 75,000 were killed in scorched-earth campaigns against indigenous communities.

label: Cuba — Cubana Flight 455 Bombing 1976
sub: CIA-trained Cuban exile Luis Posada Carriles orchestrated the bombing of Cubana de Aviación Flight 455 on October 6, 1976, killing all 73 people aboard. Posada escaped from Venezuelan prison in 1985. The US refused Venezuelan and Cuban extradition requests; he lived freely in Miami until his 2018 death.

label: Barbados — Cubana Flight 455 Crash Site
sub: Cubana Flight 455 exploded over the coast of Barbados on October 6, 1976 after a bomb planted by CIA-trained Cuban exiles detonated. All 73 passengers died, including Cuba's national fencing team. The bombing remains the deadliest terrorist attack in the Americas prior to 9/11 in terms of victims on a civilian aircraft.

label: Santa Clara — Last Battle Before Revolution
sub: Che Guevara's guerrilla forces captured Santa Clara on December 28–31, 1958, cutting Cuba in half. The decisive victory led directly to Batista's flight from Cuba. The city symbolizes the defeat of the US-backed Batista regime by the Cuban revolutionaries.

label: Port-au-Prince — Aristide Ousted 2004
sub: US Marines and diplomats facilitated President Aristide's departure on February 29, 2004. Aristide alleged he was kidnapped and put on a US military plane involuntarily. The Bush administration deployed 2,000 troops. Critics noted parallels with the 1991 coup the US had previously condemned.

label: San Salvador — Archbishop Romero Assassinated 1980
sub: Archbishop Óscar Romero was shot dead while saying Mass on March 24, 1980 by a gunman linked to Roberto D'Aubuisson's death squads. The UN Truth Commission confirmed D'Aubuisson ordered the murder. The US continued military aid to El Salvador despite the killing and the December 1980 murder of four US churchwomen.

label: Lima — Peru: Velasco Coup (1968); US Response
sub: General Juan Velasco Alvarado seized power in a 1968 coup and nationalized the US-owned International Petroleum Company. The Nixon administration considered covert action but ultimately pursued diplomatic pressure. Peru's nationalist military government pushed for non-alignment during the early Cold War.

label: Havana — US-Cuba Normalization 2015
sub: The US Embassy in Havana reopened on August 14, 2015, after 54 years of closed relations. President Obama visited Cuba in March 2016. The Trump administration reversed most normalization measures in 2017. The Biden administration maintained Cuba on the State Sponsors of Terrorism list.

label: Tegucigalpa — Honduras Drug War & Human Rights
sub: Post-2009 coup Honduras became the world's most dangerous country by homicide rate. US-backed military and police units implicated in extrajudicial killings. DEA and US military cooperation with Honduran security forces continued despite documented human rights abuses by partner units.

label: Belo Horizonte — IPES/IBAD: Pre-Coup Planning Brazil
sub: The Institute for Social Research and Studies (IPES) and the Brazilian Institute for Democratic Action (IBAD) were US-funded organizations that coordinated propaganda, lobbying, and destabilization against the Goulart government before the 1964 coup. CIA and USAID funding was confirmed by later investigations.

label: Esquipulas — Central American Peace Accords 1987
sub: The Esquipulas II Accords were signed in August 1987 by the five Central American presidents, providing a framework for ending the Contra war and other regional conflicts. Costa Rican President Oscar Arias won the Nobel Peace Prize for brokering the agreement, which the Reagan administration opposed.

label: Santiago — Operation Condor Founding Meeting 1975
sub: Operation Condor was formally established at a meeting in Santiago on November 25, 1975, organized by DINA chief Manuel Contreras. Representatives of intelligence services from Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Paraguay, and Uruguay attended. The meeting established protocols for transnational coordination of political repression.

label: Mexico City — FDR Good Neighbor Policy Consultations
sub: Mexico was a key partner in FDR's Good Neighbor Policy, which renounced US military intervention in Latin America. The 1938 Mexican oil nationalization tested the policy—the US accepted compensation rather than military intervention, marking a departure from earlier Roosevelt Corollary interventionism.
